interpretation: All architectures can detect payload modification if commitments are preserved; this is design-based detectability, not a penetration-test result.
Hash continuity and replicated evidence improve deletion detection.
This is the main scenario in which a ledger-like evidence backend has a defensible advantage.
All architectures require correct consent-state modelling; replicated evidence improves independent verifiability.
Hash-linked and replicated evidence better preserve temporal policy evidence.
A ledger-like backend is most defensible when no mutually trusted audit maintainer exists.
The privacy cost of replicated evidence must be mitigated by minimisation, pseudonymisation and retention control.
